recording_notes: Phone filming RCA monitor — same FLS skills lab as V25/V26. ~166.5s clip. Active task ~142s. Off-axis monitor capture limits exact mark visibility and hand-dominance reads.

frame_analyses.description: First instrument becomes visible in the field and the trainee begins bringing the suture/needle setup into position.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Start is controlled but not rushed; initial setup is deliberate.

frame_analyses.description: The needle is oriented on the right-hand driver and aligned toward the Penrose target.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Acceptable needle angle, with a small amount of regrasping before committing to the pass.

frame_analyses.description: The needle is driven through the Penrose near the marked target and the opposite instrument begins to receive it.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Needle transfer is controlled and tissue handling appears gentle.

frame_analyses.description: The suture is pulled through and the trainee arranges the tails in preparation for knot tying.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Pass appears successful, though tail management is somewhat wide and time-consuming.

frame_analyses.description: The first throw is being constructed over the repair site.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Appears to be a double-wrap surgeon's throw led by the right instrument, with moderate extra loop length.

frame_analyses.description: The second throw is formed and brought down over the first throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Appears to be a single throw; hand switch likely occurs here, though confidence is limited by the oblique monitor recording.

frame_analyses.description: The second throw is cinched and the knot stack is reset before the final throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Knot remains centered and secure-looking, but there is some extra time spent reorganizing tails.

frame_analyses.description: The third throw is laid and tightened to complete the knot stack.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Appears to be a single throw with switch-back to right-hand lead; final stack looks snug.

frame_analyses.description: Both suture ends have been cut short and the final knot remains in place on the Penrose.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: No avulsion is seen, and the closure remains approximated after cutting.

knot_assessments.notes: First throw appears to be a double-wrap surgeon's knot led by the right instrument; moderate confidence because the video is an off-axis recording of a monitor.
Second throw appears to be a single throw and likely alternates lead hand from the first throw, though exact hand dominance is partially obscured.
Third throw appears to be a single throw with switch-back to right-hand lead; final knot stack looks stable after tightening.

suture_placement.placement_notes: Off-axis monitor recording limits exact millimeter judgment. Placement appears close to the intended targets.

drain_assessment.assessment_note: No avulsion seen and the closure remains approximated after cutting.

technique_summary: Overall performance was controlled and completed well within the 600-second limit, with a secure-looking final knot and no drain avulsion. The main inefficiency was excess suture management between throws and before cutting, which added more time than the actual tissue pass. Final closure appears well approximated without an obvious residual gap.
improvement_suggestions: Reduce excess tail handling after the needle pass and between throws; this was the biggest source of lost time.
Make the hand switch between throws more deliberate and visually distinct so the alternating pattern is unmistakable.
Bring the scissors in sooner after the third throw and cut promptly once the knot is seated to shorten the final phase.
strengths: Needle passage was controlled and the drain remained intact without avulsion throughout the task.
The final knot stack appears snug, the slit looks well approximated, and both tails were successfully cut intracorporeally.
comparison_notes: Strong score agreement with Claude V27 (ΔFLS 2.0, Δtime 2.0s). Both teachers identify V27 as a fatigue regression from V26's series best back into the V21–V25 plateau band, with no avulsion and a secure final knot. Phase-attribution disagreement: GPT-4o concentrates lost time in inter-throw tail management, while Claude attributes the regression specifically to throw 3 (27s) and the cutting phase (53s). Placement penalty disagreement: Claude 4.0mm (2.0 per mark) vs GPT-4o 2.0mm (1.0 per mark); both low-confidence due to monitor capture.
